title: AI and Labor: Economist Position Tracker
subtitle: Longitudinal monitoring of five economists' positions on AI's labor-market impact — checked every six months.
scope: McAfee, Autor, Acemoglu, Brynjolfsson, Aghion — plus empirical predictions, indicator dashboard, consensus map, and a period-grouped citation feed.
sourceCoverage: Seeded from the April 2026 synthesis at /Users/rene/projects/economists-ai-labor.html.
objective: Track the evolution of expert consensus on AI's labor-market impact as evidence accumulates and positions shift.
successCondition: A reader at any monitoring period sees current stance per expert, live resolution of each prediction, latest indicator values, and what shifted since the prior period — without re-reading the underlying source material.

objectiveFacets.name: Current positions accurate
objectiveFacets.description: Each expert's stance reflects their most recent published work in the current period.

objectiveFacets.name: Predictions tracked
objectiveFacets.description: Every 'what to watch' claim has a live resolution status with evidence accumulating.

objectiveFacets.name: Indicators current
objectiveFacets.description: The numerical dashboard matches the latest prints, each with an as-of date and source.

objectiveFacets.name: Divergence map current
objectiveFacets.description: The consensus/divergence axes reflect current positions; prior-period positions preserved.

objectiveFacets.name: Period attribution
objectiveFacets.description: Every card update is attributable to a named monitoring period.

periods.summary: Baseline — five positions captured from April 2026 sources; 4 predictions open; 15 sources ingested.

invariants.name: Expert stance must cite period source
invariants.statement: Every expert-position card must cite at least one source dated in the current or prior period.

invariants.name: Every prediction carries a resolution status
invariants.statement: Every prediction card must carry a resolution (planned / partial / ready / blocked) with rationale.

invariants.name: Every indicator is dated
invariants.statement: Every indicator card must have a latestValue with an as-of date and a source.

invariants.name: Every source has a publication date
invariants.statement: Every citation card must carry a publishedAt field.

invariants.name: Period note exists when period closes
invariants.statement: When the current period has closed, the period note for that period must exist.

invariants.name: Divergence map covers all tracked thinkers
invariants.statement: The divergence map must include position cards for every thinker in the experts section.

callouts.title: How to read this doc
items: Experts is the landing view — each card is one tracked public-intellectual stance.
Predictions tracks the 'what to watch' claims from the April 2026 synthesis as a proof ladder.
Indicators is the numeric dashboard — each card is one metric with a latest-value and a trend.
Divergence map plots each thinker on two axes; prior-period position is preserved on each card.
Sources is period-grouped; additions to a prior period are allowed but flagged.
Period note is one card per check-in summarising what shifted and what matters next.

sections.title: Expert positions
sections.rationale: Five tracked voices whose published positions on AI and labor move over time. One card per expert, with evolution since last period surfaced as a first-class field.

data.name: Andrew McAfee
data.affiliation: MIT Sloan · Initiative on the Digital Economy · The Geek Way (Substack)
data.coreView: Macro labor-market signal from AI is still faint. 'Robots take the jobs' framings keep failing the data. Jobs are bundles of tasks; AI unbundles some but rarely a whole occupation.
whatTheyEmphasise: Weak-link economics: output is bottlenecked by the slowest-improving tasks, so dramatic task-level automation produces only gradual macro acceleration.
Historical pattern holds: ATMs, radiologists, translators — each time the 'this one kills the jobs' prediction has failed.
Reshaping beats replacement: 'How many jobs will AI kill?' is the wrong question.

notes.text: Richmond Fed survey: near-zero average 2026 employment effect from AI.
Call-centre study: +15% productivity on average, +30% for least-skilled workers. Expert forecast: LFP at 55% by 2050 (from 62%).

data.name: David Autor
data.affiliation: MIT Economics · Shaping the Future of Work Initiative
data.coreView: The 40-year hollowing-out of middle-skill work was driven by automation of routine tasks. AI is different — it can extend expert judgment to non-experts. If policy directs the technology well, AI could rebuild the middle class rather than finish dismantling it.
whatTheyEmphasise: AI co-pilots democratise expertise previously gated behind years of training.
Wages are already telling the story: AI-exposed computer-systems-design sector wages up ~16.7% since fall 2022, vs. 7.5% nationally.
Shape the technology, don't just adapt to it. Wage insurance flagged as promising for displaced workers.

notes.text: Key quote: 'The risk is not running out of jobs — we're running out of workers. The risk is the devaluation of expertise.'

data.name: Daron Acemoglu
data.affiliation: MIT Economics · 2024 Nobel laureate · co-author, Power and Progress
data.coreView: Current AI is aimed at 'so-so automation' — cutting labor costs without big productivity wins — and that's a choice, not destiny. Macro estimates: next decade of AI adds ~0.55–0.71% to TFP and 1.1–1.6% to GDP. Far below industry hype.
whatTheyEmphasise: Create new tasks, don't just substitute. Historical wage gains came from technology that created work, not just displaced it.
April 2026 co-authored paper with Autor and Johnson: pure automation 'commodifies human expertise, rendering it less valuable and potentially superfluous.'
Pro-worker AI is possible — but needs redirection of innovation, plus regulation and new institutions.

notes.text: Key quote: 'We currently have the wrong direction for AI. We're using it too much for automation and not enough for providing expertise and information to workers.'
10-year TFP gain from AI: 0.55–0.71%. 10-year GDP gain: 1.1–1.6%. Implied annual productivity: ~0.05%.

data.name: Erik Brynjolfsson
data.affiliation: Stanford Digital Economy Lab · co-author, The Second Machine Age
data.coreView: US productivity hit ~2.7% in 2025, roughly double the decade average. The investment phase (intangibles, workflow redesign, retraining) is giving way to a harvest phase where measured output finally shows up. Meanwhile a two-sided squeeze on workers is visible: entry-level white-collar hit from above, minimum-wage-driven robot adoption from below.
whatTheyEmphasise: From the top: his 2025 study found employment for 22–25-year-olds in the most AI-exposed jobs down ~13% on a relative basis.
From the bottom: February 2026 paper shows minimum-wage increases accelerate industrial-robot adoption on factory floors.
The Turing Trap: if AI is optimised to imitate humans, the value of human labor falls by construction. Amplification beats imitation — ethically and economically.

notes.text: Key phrase: 'Treat humans as an end, not just a means to an end.'

data.name: Philippe Aghion
data.affiliation: LSE & Collège de France · 2025 Nobel laureate (with Howitt & Mokyr)
data.coreView: AI is the next wave of Schumpeterian creative destruction. Estimates put AI's contribution to productivity growth at 0.8–1.3 pp/year over the next decade (median ~0.68 pp in a task-based approach). Empirical evidence so far does not support 'AI destroys jobs' — conditional on the right institutions.
whatTheyEmphasise: Administrative and clerical jobs with high task-substitutability are the most at risk.
Job creation happens via new ideas. AI lowers the cost of ideation, which creates work as well as destroys it.
The Danish flexicurity model (flexible labor + 90% wage replacement up to 2 years + state retraining) is the reference design.
Risk if institutions fail: AI concentrates market power rather than diffusing gains.

notes.text: Key quote: 'If you have competition, education and good flexicurity, we will make the best of the AI revolution. It will be one way Europe can revive.'

sections.title: Empirical predictions
sections.rationale: The 'what to watch in 2026–2027' claims from the synthesis rendered as a proof ladder. Each rung resolves as evidence accumulates: planned = pending, partial = mixed, ready = confirmed, blocked = refuted.

data.name: The 2025 US productivity jump persists into 2026+

notes.text: Source: Brynjolfsson Fortune piece (Feb 2026) and 2025 BLS prints. Baseline 2025 TFP ~2.7%.
Resolution criterion: if 2026 H2 TFP prints hold above ~2.0% with AI-exposed sector contribution visible in component breakdowns, confirmed. If 2026 retraces to <1.5%, refuted.

data.name: Entry-level employment for 22–25yo in AI-exposed white-collar work keeps falling

notes.text: Baseline: Brynjolfsson 2025 study — ~13% relative decline on most AI-exposed.
Resolution: if H2 2026 and H1 2027 continue the negative relative trend, confirmed. If the cohort stabilises or recovers, refuted.

data.name: Wage dispersion within AI-exposed occupations widens (expertise premium rises)

notes.text: Autor baseline: AI-exposed computer-systems-design wages up 16.7% since fall 2022 vs. 7.5% nationally.

Resolution: if within-occupation top-decile/median wage ratio rises in AI-exposed roles across 2026 and 2027, confirmed. If compression is observed instead, refuted — and Acemoglu's 'commodification of expertise' thesis gains.

data.name: A major economy adopts flexicurity-style wage insurance for AI-displaced workers

notes.text: Aghion reference design. Autor has flagged wage insurance as promising in US policy commentary.
Resolution: confirmed if any OECD economy passes a wage-insurance program explicitly motivated by AI displacement by end of 2027. Refuted otherwise.

sections.title: Indicator dashboard
sections.rationale: Numeric indicators tracked across monitoring periods. First-version workaround for numeric status: carry the value as text and use the categorical `trend` status for direction.

data.name: US Total Factor Productivity growth
data.forecast: 0.68–1.3 pp/yr from AI alone (Aghion); near-zero AI contribution (Acemoglu)

notes.text: Brynjolfsson Fortune (Feb 2026); Aghion & Bunel SF Fed paper; Acemoglu NBER w32487.

data.name: Employment: 22–25yo in most AI-exposed occupations (relative)
data.forecast: If AI-capability jumps continue at Dec 2025 pace, further declines expected

notes.text: Brynjolfsson 2025 study; replicated in Dallas Fed (Feb 2026).

data.name: Wage growth in AI-exposed computer-systems-design sector vs. national
data.forecast: Continued expertise premium expansion if co-pilot productivity effects compound

notes.text: Autor NBER w32140; Issues in Science and Technology interview.

data.name: OECD adoption of AI-motivated wage insurance (count)
data.forecast: At least one proposal expected in 2026–2027 per Aghion and Autor commentary

notes.text: Denmark's existing flexicurity is the reference design but predates the AI-motivated framing. Counter as adoption only if a program is passed with AI displacement as a named motivation.

sections.title: Consensus & divergence map
sections.rationale: Two-axis placement of each thinker. Axis X: magnitude of AI impact on labor markets (weak → strong). Axis Y: stance on institutional role (market-led → institution-led). Prior-period position preserved per card so movement is visible.

data.name: Andrew McAfee
data.axisY: mid market-led (notes policy but emphasises firm-level task unbundling)

data.name: Daron Acemoglu
data.axisX: weak on current trajectory (so-so automation, ~0.05% annual productivity)
data.axisY: strong institution-led (redirection, regulation, new institutions required)

data.name: Erik Brynjolfsson
data.axisX: strong (J-curve bottom crossed, harvest phase visible)

sections.title: Period notes
sections.rationale: One card per monitoring period summarising what shifted, what resolved, and what matters for the next period.

notes.text: First monitoring period. Five expert stances captured from April 2026 sources. Four empirical predictions declared (pending). Four indicators dashboarded (baseline). Five thinkers placed on the divergence map (no prior period to compare to). Fifteen sources ingested.

notes.title: Focus for next period (2026-H2)
items: Watch H1 2026 TFP prints — does the 2.7% 2025 level hold?
Cohort data for 22–25yo in AI-exposed roles — continues falling, or stabilising?
Any public OECD policy mentions of wage insurance motivated by AI.
Acemoglu vs. Aghion productivity estimates — do late-2026 papers narrow or widen the gap?
